caveats: a: The BLS source is four-digit NAICS 611500 and includes delivery models excluded from the lens.
a: The estimate treats assistive use as exposure only when it can substitute labor or avoid hiring, not merely improve course quality.
a: The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization divisor, with the stated vintage and basis limitations.
rho: Census data are all-industry and the survey question broadened in November 2025 from production to any business function.
rho: Guidance shows permissibility and intended uses, not productivity or avoided hiring.
rho: State-by-state approval and recordkeeping rules can slow deployment even when the instructional technology is ready.
e: No source measures the share of $1-10M EBITDA 611519 firms that matches the narrowed lens.
e: Schools often teach multiple subjects or combine online theory with physical practice, making binary classification difficult.
e: The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate using size-class data and an education-sector margin rather than observed firm EBITDA.
s5: The source is economy-wide, based on 2018 owner data, and represents responding owners rather than firms.
s5: Control transfers that lose required approvals, students, instructors, or operating continuity do not qualify.
s5: Deaths, closures, minority investments, and internal reorganizations are excluded.
q: The regulatory sources cover real-estate education in two states, not every subject in the lens.
q: Approval barriers can support price in some states, while online delivery and public or employer substitutes can compress it elsewhere.
q: The estimate excludes volume loss and implementation difficulty, which are represented in other primitives.
d5: IPEDS covers Title IV institutions and excludes many short, noncredit, non-Title-IV providers in the lens.
d5: Teacher employment is not student demand and includes public secondary and postsecondary institutions outside 611519.
d5: The mixed subject portfolio can diverge sharply with local licensing, housing, technology, hospitality, and security labor markets.
o: Real-estate rules illustrate regulated credential training but do not apply to every subject in the narrowed lens.
o: An approved operator can become much thinner and more software-centric while still counting as operator-required demand.
o: Rules can change to permit direct assessment or self-service, especially in subjects without safety-sensitive practice.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Mostly posted tuition or fixed per-enrollment and per-cohort fees paid by individuals or employers, with repeat demand from new entrants, continuing-education requirements, employer upskilling, and course updates.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 611519 combines asset-light online or classroom credential schools with fleet-, shop-, kitchen-, laboratory-, and clinical-practice schools whose labor substitution, capital intensity, and operator requirements are too different for one coherent screen. The lens keeps the classroom/online credential model and excludes equipment- and supervised-practice-intensive delivery solely to make the operating model coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Truck, bus, heavy-equipment, mechanical, culinary, health-clinical, and other schools whose core delivery requires fleets, shops, kitchens, clinical labs, or supervised physical practice; captive employer schools; public institutions; degree programs; and one-off grant vehicles without recurring customers.
lens.included_activities: Privately controlled schools repeatedly selling primarily classroom, webinar, correspondence, or online job-specific instruction and certification preparation to individuals or external employers, including real-estate, insurance, banking, security, broadcasting, graphic-arts, bartending, and other asset-light vocational subjects.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for privately owned NAICS 611500, which includes all technical and trade schools rather than the narrowed asset-light 611519 lens, and does not measure AI task exposure. I mapped the occupation mix to current task families and approximately wage-weighted them: course drafting and updating, explanations, formative assessment, learner support, marketing, records, scheduling, and routine administration are exposed; live facilitation, regulated final assessment, counseling, sales conversion, and accountable oversight are less exposed. Because the lens excludes physical-practice schools, the broader occupation mix probably understates exposure for the retained population.
caveats: The BLS source is four-digit NAICS 611500 and includes delivery models excluded from the lens.
The estimate treats assistive use as exposure only when it can substitute labor or avoid hiring, not merely improve course quality.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization divisor, with the stated vintage and basis limitations.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census from a representative sample of asset-light 611519 schools, tagged to O*NET tasks and current automation status and separated by delivery mode, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Private technical and trade schools employed 43.94% of workers in educational occupations, 14.34% in office and administrative support, 10.49% in business and financial operations, 10.60% in management, and 3.42% in community and social service occupations in May 2023. The retained schools concentrate work in digital content, instruction, learner communication, and records, making roughly half of wage-weighted current tasks exposed while preserving substantial human work in motivation, judgment, approval, and customer acquisition.

d5.bridge: IPEDS fall enrollment at Title IV less-than-two-year private for-profit institutions and BLS career-technical teacher projections cover broader and different populations than constant-price demand for the narrowed 611519 service basket. I use the decline from 318,341 such students in 2010 to 216,948 in 2023 as a structural caution, while the 1% projected growth in postsecondary career-technical teachers from 2024 to 2034 supports stabilization. IPEDS omits many non-Title-IV licensing and continuing-education schools and therefore may overstate decline for the retained lens; teacher employment may understate digital enrollment because online schools can scale without proportional teachers.
caveats: IPEDS covers Title IV institutions and excludes many short, noncredit, non-Title-IV providers in the lens.
Teacher employment is not student demand and includes public secondary and postsecondary institutions outside 611519.
The mixed subject portfolio can diverge sharply with local licensing, housing, technology, hospitality, and security labor markets.
d5.change_evidence: Quarterly enrollments, completions, real tuition, continuing-education renewals, employer cohort purchases, and new-license counts from IPEDS, state licensing boards, accrediting agencies, and a provider cohort through 2031 would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: The broad private for-profit short-program channel remains sizable but below its 2010 enrollment level, and BLS projects essentially flat postsecondary career-technical teacher employment. Licensing and recurring continuing education provide a floor, while employer self-training, free online content, software-led instruction, changing career demand, and regulation create downside. The base is a modest 4% five-year contraction rather than extrapolating the long historical decline.

caveats: No source measures the share of $1-10M EBITDA 611519 firms that matches the narrowed lens.
Schools often teach multiple subjects or combine online theory with physical practice, making binary classification difficult.
The frozen firm count is a margin-bridged estimate using size-class data and an education-sector margin rather than observed firm EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of the 173 estimated LMM firms using 2022 Economic Census product lines, state school licenses, facility and equipment profiles, ownership, customer type, recurring revenue, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The 611519 definition spans bartending, broadcasting, computer repair, graphic arts, real estate, truck driving, specialized military, health, culinary, security, and many other schools. The coherent lens excludes the substantial set requiring vehicles, shops, kitchens, laboratories, clinical practice, or captive delivery, then retains privately controlled external providers with repeat cohorts or recurring continuing education. Without a current product-line split by firm and EBITDA, an estimated 42% of the frozen LMM count fits.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 611519 ($3,782,281,434) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 611519 ($8,375,128,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1657 (margins.json[61]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Education EBITDA/Sales 16.57%); revenue/employee $144,081 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 611519); class <5: 2,296 firms, avg revenue/firm $265,126 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,931 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 669 firms, avg revenue/firm $750,668 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $124,386 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 456 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,636,300 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $271,135 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 378 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,609,249 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $763,753 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 94 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,963,830 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,473,707 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $35,508,253 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,883,718 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 173

caveats: Real-estate rules illustrate regulated credential training but do not apply to every subject in the narrowed lens.
An approved operator can become much thinner and more software-centric while still counting as operator-required demand.
Rules can change to permit direct assessment or self-service, especially in subjects without safety-sensitive practice.
o.change_evidence: State-board data on approved-provider counts, direct exam eligibility, AI-only or self-study acceptance, school closure and revocation rates, enrollment by delivery mode, and student willingness to buy unbundled software would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: The retained courses are highly structured and specialized and often lead to job-specific certification. In regulated fields, an approved school remains responsible for instructors, engagement, monitored assessment, completion certificates, timely reporting, and current course materials even when delivery is online. AI can supply much content, practice, tutoring, and administration, and nonregulated students can self-serve, but approval, records, assessment integrity, brand, and learner support should keep an accountable operator in most year-five quantity.

caveats: The regulatory sources cover real-estate education in two states, not every subject in the lens.
Approval barriers can support price in some states, while online delivery and public or employer substitutes can compress it elsewhere.
The estimate excludes volume loss and implementation difficulty, which are represented in other primitives.
q.change_evidence: A provider-level panel of list and realized tuition, discounts, refund rates, customer acquisition cost, renewal terms, gross margin, and labor cost before and after automation, segmented by state and course, would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Posted tuition and fixed per-enrollment fees let a school initially retain savings from course production, support, grading, and administration. Over five years, online delivery makes prices and alternatives easy to compare, low marginal costs invite entry and discounting, employer contracts renew, and regulators approve multiple providers and delivery modes. Savings should therefore be shared substantially through lower prices, promotions, added support, or higher course-update frequency rather than fully retained.

rho.bridge: Census measures current and expected AI use for all U.S. employer businesses, while federal education guidance identifies potential applications rather than realized labor implementation in 611519. I translate these signals into five-year implementation for asset-light career schools. Their digital course materials and structured workflows should raise implementation relative to the national firm average, but small-firm systems, course-approval rules, assessment integrity, learner variability, privacy, and human review reduce labor realization. The national proxy likely understates adoption potential; policy use cases likely overstate realized savings.
caveats: Census data are all-industry and the survey question broadened in November 2025 from production to any business function.
Guidance shows permissibility and intended uses, not productivity or avoided hiring.
State-by-state approval and recordkeeping rules can slow deployment even when the instructional technology is ready.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of 611519 schools measuring workflow-level deployment, labor hours per enrollment, completion and exam-pass outcomes, course-approval exceptions, hallucination defects, refunds, and human override rates would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Across U.S. businesses, AI use in any business function was 17%-20% from December 2025 to May 2026 and expected use was 20%-23%. Education guidance explicitly identifies personalized learning, differentiated instruction, classroom efficiency, lower administrative burden, and teacher training. Over five years, the standardized, text-heavy retained model can implement more than half of exposed opportunity, but regulated content and assessments and customer-facing quality controls prevent full conversion.

s5.bridge: The Census owner-age statistic covers responding owners of U.S. employer businesses rather than eligible 611519 LMM schools and measures age, not qualifying transfers. I use it only as a succession-pressure indicator, then reduce it for five-year timing, multi-owner continuity, closures, internal transfers, approval nonassignability, and deals that do not preserve operating control. The broad age proxy may overstate transfer propensity for institutionally owned schools and understate it for long-held founder-led local providers.
caveats: The source is economy-wide, based on 2018 owner data, and represents responding owners rather than firms.
Control transfers that lose required approvals, students, instructors, or operating continuity do not qualify.
Deaths, closures, minority investments, and internal reorganizations are excluded.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal and ownership histories from PitchBook, Axial, GF Data, BizBuySell, state vocational-school regulators, and accreditor change-of-control notices for 611519 firms from 2016-2026 would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: The 2019 Annual Business Survey visualization reported 51% of responding U.S. employer-business owners were age 55 or older. Asset-light schools can be founder-led and operationally transferable, but owner age does not ensure a sale, and course, school, Title IV, or state approvals can complicate change of control. A 24% five-year base allows meaningful succession without treating all retirement exposure as transactions.

principal_driver: Standardized digital course, assessment-support, learner-service, and administrative workflows create a broad implementable AI surface.
principal_weakness: Online commoditization and mixed-to-declining enrollment evidence can force efficiency gains into lower prices rather than durable retained benefit.
